intro-1.plaintext: To make your site editable with Hydra you load hydra.js in your frontend and configure it with your block types. Then you progressively add HTML annotations to enable visual editing features.

children.text: To make your site editable with Hydra you load hydra.js in your frontend and configure it with your block types. Then you progressively add HTML annotations to enable visual editing features.

bridge-text-t0.plaintext: Include hydra.js in your frontend and call initBridge() to create a two-way communication channel between Hydra and your frontend. The bridge handles authentication, page navigation syncing, and block data transfer.

children.text: Include hydra.js in your frontend and call
to create a two-way communication channel between Hydra and your frontend. The bridge handles authentication, page navigation syncing, and block data transfer.

tabs.label: JavaScript

bridge-text-t2.plaintext: The page.schema.properties object configures which blocks fields your page has. The default field is blocks_layout. Each entry specifies a display title and which block types are allowed.

children.text: object configures which blocks fields your page has. The default field is
. Each entry specifies a display title and which block types are allowed.

multi-text-t0.plaintext: Pages can have multiple blocks fields for different areas (e.g., header, content, footer). Each shows as a separate section in the sidebar when no block is selected:

children.text: Pages can have multiple blocks fields for different areas (e.g., header, content, footer). Each shows as a separate section in the sidebar when no block is selected:

tabs.label: JavaScript

pathtransform-text-t0.plaintext: If your frontend embeds state in the URL path (like pagination), you need to tell hydra.js how to transform the frontend path to the API/admin path. Otherwise, the admin will try to navigate to URLs that don't exist in the CMS.

children.text: If your frontend embeds state in the URL path (like pagination), you need to tell hydra.js how to transform the frontend path to the API/admin path. Otherwise, the admin will try to navigate to URLs that don't exist in the CMS.

tabs.label: JavaScript

pathtransform-text-t2.plaintext: The pathToApiPath function is called whenever hydra.js sends a PATH_CHANGE message to the admin, allowing your frontend to strip or transform URL segments that are frontend-specific (like pagination, filters, or other client-side state).

children.text: function is called whenever hydra.js sends a PATH_CHANGE message to the admin, allowing your frontend to strip or transform URL segments that are frontend-specific (like pagination, filters, or other client-side state).

callback-text-t0.plaintext: Register onEditChange to receive live content updates as the user edits. Your frontend re-renders in realtime using the same data format as the Plone REST API.

children.text: to receive live content updates as the user edits. Your frontend re-renders in realtime using the same data format as the Plone REST API.

tabs.label: JavaScript

callback-text-t2.plaintext: Since the data structure is the same as returned by the Plone REST API, it's normally easy to re-render your page dynamically using the same code your frontend used to render the page initially. With onEditChange registered, editors get: Changes appear on the frontend as they type in the sidebarAdding and removing blocks shows immediately in the previewDrag and drop block reordering with live previewA '+' icon on the frontend to add blocks after the current block Note: For live updates you need a frontend deployed as SPA or hybrid rather than SSR or SSG. If your production deploy is SSG/SSR, deploy another edit-only preview frontend in SPA mode.

children.text: Since the data structure is the same as returned by the Plone REST API, it's normally easy to re-render your page dynamically using the same code your frontend used to render the page initially.
Changes appear on the frontend as they type in the sidebar
Adding and removing blocks shows immediately in the preview
Drag and drop block reordering with live preview
A '+' icon on the frontend to add blocks after the current block
Note: For live updates you need a frontend deployed as SPA or hybrid rather than SSR or SSG. If your production deploy is SSG/SSR, deploy another edit-only preview frontend in SPA mode.

custom-text-t0.plaintext: Define custom block types directly in your frontend configuration via the blocks option in initBridge. No Volto plugin deployment required. Each block type needs an id, title, and a blockSchema with its field properties.

children.text: Define custom block types directly in your frontend configuration via the
option in initBridge. No Volto plugin deployment required. Each block type needs an id, title, and a blockSchema with its field properties.

tabs.label: JavaScript

custom-text-t2.plaintext: Child block types (like 'slide' above) must be defined at the top level of blocks. You can also: Set restricted: true to hide a block from the block chooser (only usable as child blocks)Set mostUsed: true to pin a block to the top of the chooserSet sidebarSchemaOnly: true to use only the schema form in the sidebar (no custom edit component)Use fieldsets in the schema to organize fields into tabs

children.text: Child block types (like 'slide' above) must be defined at the top level of blocks. You can also:
to hide a block from the block chooser (only usable as child blocks)
to pin a block to the top of the chooser
to use only the schema form in the sidebar (no custom edit component)
in the schema to organize fields into tabs

tabs.label: JavaScript

enhancers-text-t2.plaintext: skiplogic: Conditionally show/hide fields based on other field values. field: Field to check (use ../field for parent/page fields)Operators: is, isNot, isSet, isNotSet, gt, gte, lt, lte

children.text: : Conditionally show/hide fields based on other field values.
: Field to check (use ../field for parent/page fields)
Operators: is, isNot, isSet, isNotSet, gt, gte, lt, lte

conversion-text-t0.plaintext: If a block type has a fieldMappings defined it will enable a "Convert to..." UI action. You specify either conversions to a specific type, or to a generic search result schema (@id, title, preview-image, description):

children.text: If a block type has a
defined it will enable a "Convert to..." UI action. You specify either conversions to a specific type, or to a generic search result schema (@id, title, preview-image, description):

tabs.label: JavaScript

conversion-text-t2.plaintext: Transitive conversions are performed automatically by using paths through intermediate types (e.g., hero -> teaser -> image). Any fields that don't match will still be kept in the data so if the block is converted back that data will reappear.

children.text: Transitive conversions are performed automatically by using paths through intermediate types (e.g., hero -> teaser -> image). Any fields that don't match will still be kept in the data so if the block is converted back that data will reappear.

inherit-heading.plaintext: Synchronised Block Types in a Container

children.text: Synchronised Block Types in a Container

inherit-text-t0.plaintext: You might want one container type that holds different block types but constrains them to all be the same type with synchronised settings. A field on the parent lets the editor select the type and all blocks get converted using fieldMappings:

children.text: You might want one container type that holds different block types but constrains them to all be the same type with synchronised settings. A field on the parent lets the editor select the type and all blocks get converted using fieldMappings:

tabs.label: JavaScript

inherit-text-t2.plaintext: inheritSchemaFrom: Parent inherits schema from selected child type. When the type field changes, child blocks sync to new type.typeField: Field name for selecting child type (e.g., 'variation')defaultsField: Field name for storing inherited defaults (e.g., 'itemDefaults')blocksField: Which blocks field the sub-blocks are in. Set to ".." to use the parent's allowedBlocks.filterConvertibleFrom: Only allow selecting a block type which can convert from the specified type.childBlockConfig: Child hides fields except editableFields when inside a parent with inheritSchemaFrom.

children.text: : Parent inherits schema from selected child type. When the type field changes, child blocks sync to new type.
: Field name for selecting child type (e.g., 'variation')
: Field name for storing inherited defaults (e.g., 'itemDefaults')
: Which blocks field the sub-blocks are in. Set to ".." to use the parent's allowedBlocks.
: Only allow selecting a block type which can convert from the specified type.
: Child hides fields except editableFields when inside a parent with inheritSchemaFrom.

annotations-text-t0.plaintext: Add data attributes to your rendered HTML to enable progressively richer visual editing: data-block-uid="blockId" — Click-to-select blocks. Hydra.js adds click handlers and shows a blue outline and Quanta toolbar on selected blocks.data-edit-text="fieldName" — Inline text editing. For simple text, click and type directly. For rich text (slate widget), select text to apply formatting via the Quanta toolbar.data-edit-media="fieldName" — Visual media uploading. Editors can upload, pick or drag-and-drop images directly onto the element.data-edit-link="fieldName" — Link editing. Click behaviour is replaced with a link picker to select content, enter an external URL, or open the link. Example of a fully annotated slide block:

children.text: Add data attributes to your rendered HTML to enable progressively richer visual editing:
— Click-to-select blocks. Hydra.js adds click handlers and shows a blue outline and Quanta toolbar on selected blocks.
— Inline text editing. For simple text, click and type directly. For rich text (slate widget), select text to apply formatting via the Quanta toolbar.
— Visual media uploading. Editors can upload, pick or drag-and-drop images directly onto the element.
— Link editing. Click behaviour is replaced with a link picker to select content, enter an external URL, or open the link.
Example of a fully annotated slide block:

comment-text-t2.plaintext: Attributes without selectors apply to the root element: block-uid=xxxAttributes with selectors target child elements: edit-text=title(.card-title)Closing <!-- /hydra --> marks end of scopeSelf-closing <!-- hydra block-uid=xxx /--> applies only to next sibling element Supported attributes: block-uid, block-readonly, edit-text, edit-link, edit-media, block-add

children.text: Attributes without selectors apply to the root element: block-uid=xxx
Attributes with selectors target child elements: edit-text=title(.card-title)
Self-closing <!-- hydra block-uid=xxx /--> applies only to next sibling element
Supported attributes: block-uid, block-readonly, edit-text, edit-link, edit-media, block-add

pathsyntax-text-t0.plaintext: The data-edit-text|edit-media|edit-link attributes support Unix-style paths to edit fields outside the current block: fieldName — edit the block's own field (default)../fieldName — edit the parent block's field../../fieldName — edit the grandparent's field/fieldName — edit the page metadata field

children.text: The data-edit-text|edit-media|edit-link attributes support Unix-style paths to edit fields outside the current block:

nodeid-text-t0.plaintext: When rendering Slate nodes to DOM, your renderer must follow these rules for data-node-id: Element nodes (p, strong, em, etc.) must have data-node-id matching the Slate node's nodeIdWrapper elements — If you add extra wrapper elements around a Slate node, ALL wrappers must have the same data-node-id as the inner element hydra.js uses node-ids to map between Slate's data model and your DOM. When restoring cursor position after formatting changes, it walks your DOM counting Slate children.

children.text: When rendering Slate nodes to DOM, your renderer must follow these rules for
Element nodes (p, strong, em, etc.) must have data-node-id matching the Slate node's nodeId
Wrapper elements — If you add extra wrapper elements around a Slate node, ALL wrappers must have the same data-node-id as the inner element
hydra.js uses node-ids to map between Slate's data model and your DOM. When restoring cursor position after formatting changes, it walks your DOM counting Slate children.

tabs.label: HTML
tabs.code: Valid wrapper pattern: bold Both elements have the same node-id, so they count as one Slate child. Invalid (missing node-id on wrapper): bold This breaks cursor positioning because hydra.js can't correlate DOM structure to Slate structure.
